summary:
Tang Dynasty poetry (618–907 AD) was the supreme achievement of classical Chinese literary culture, elevating verse to the central art form of Chinese civilization in ways that persisted for over a millennium. Poetry in Tang China was not merely an elite pastime but a social institution: the civil service examination tested poetic composition, government officials communicated through verse, friends commemorated parting with poems, and a poet's reputation could translate directly into court preferment. This unprecedented institutionalization of poetry as the mark of educated civilization produced both enormous volume (the Complete Tang Poems anthology contains over 48,000 poems by nearly 2,300 authors) and extraordinary quality.

The Tang poetic tradition encompassed diverse forms and sensibilities. The 'regulated verse' (lüshi) form — eight lines in five or seven characters each, with strict tonal patterns and mandatory couplet parallelism in the middle four lines — achieved technical refinement under the great poets of the High Tang period (c. 712–765 AD), including [[event:2957|Wu Zetian's]] court poets and the giants Du Fu and Li Bai, whose work is covered under the [[event:2958|Tang Poetry Golden Age]]. The late Tang period (c. 827–907 AD) produced its own masters, including Du Mu and Li Shangyin, whose ornate, allusive style contrasted with the directness of High Tang verse. Wang Wei pioneered the integration of Buddhist meditation philosophy with landscape poetry, creating verse so visually precise that later critics said his poems 'contain paintings.' The Tang poetic tradition was inherited by the Song Dynasty, transmitted through Korea, Japan, and Vietnam as the model for literary culture, and remains the standard against which Chinese poetry is measured.
